Digital AI Lexicon

The Digital AI Lexicon is a searchable digital database on key AI governance terms present in regulatory frameworks on AI in the European Union and the United States.

The Digital AI Lexicon takes the EU's AI Act as a reference point, juxtaposing and organising the other jurisdictions' terms based on the EU regulatory framework for AI. It covers a total of 43 terms across 12 regulatory frameworks and relevant guidance documents. For each term, it catalogues the definition, scope, enforcement context, and authoritative source, with side-by-side EU–U.S. comparisons and interpretive notes.

The Digital AI Lexicon was produced by the Centre for European Policy Studies (CEPS) in the context of the EU-U.S. Trade and Technology Dialogue (TTD), funded by the European Commission. Study reference: NDICI FPN FPI /2022/432-762.

Regulations

Includes enacted laws and amendments across six jurisdictions. Pending bills are excluded. "In effect" indicates the law has been enacted; enforcement dates may be future-dated.

European Union ·2 California ·2 Colorado ·2 New York ·2 Texas ·1 Utah ·2

European Union

3 frameworks
Artificial Intelligence Act (2024)
Regulation (EU) 2024/1689
In effect

The AI Act (hereafter, AIA) puts in place obligations for providers and deployers of AI systems in the EU, drawing on a risk-based classification system that includes unacceptable risk (prohibited practices), minimal risk, limited risk, and high-risk AI systems. It also regulates AI models, namely general-purpose AI models and general-purpose AI models with systemic risks (over 10²⁵ FLOPs in training compute), with specific obligations in place for these categories of providers.

The AIA applies to providers and deployers located outside the EU where the output of the AI system is used in the Union (Article 2). As such, providers and deployers located in a third country can fall within the scope of the AIA. This contrasts with the U.S. state legal frameworks analysed herein, which generally apply only to activity within the relevant state (with some variation in nexus tests).

Effective: 1 Aug 2024 Key provisions: Art. 3 · Art. 17 · Art. 52 · Annex III
Guidelines on the scope of the obligations for general-purpose AI models established by AIA (2025)
C(2025) 7719 final
Interpretive guidance

These guidelines (hereafter, GL) provide clarification on the definition and scope of obligations in place in the AIA related to GPAI models and GPAI models with systemic risks (GPAISR).

Effective: 2 Aug 2025
Code of Practice for General-purpose AI Models (2025)
Under Article 56 AI Act
Voluntary guidance

Formulated under Article 56 of the AIA, the Code of Practice (hereafter, CoP) provides operational guidance for providers of General-Purpose AI (GPAI) models to demonstrate compliance with the obligations listed under the AIA (Articles 53 and 55). Whereas the CoP is not legally binding, signatories of the CoP commit to implementing the measures outlined herein. The CoP comprises three chapters: 1) the copyright chapter (hereafter, CoP CC), the transparency chapter (hereafter, CoP TC), as well as safety and security chapter for GPAI models with systemic risk (hereafter, CoP SSC).

Effective: 2 Aug 2025

California

2 frameworks
SB 53 (2025) — Transparency in Frontier Artificial Intelligence (TFAI)
In effect

This bill puts in place safety, reporting and risk management obligations for providers of frontier models (over 10²⁶ FLOPs in training compute). Obligations differ according to the revenue of the frontier developer, with those having over $500M in annual gross revenues being required to put in place a Frontier AI Framework for risk management.

Effective: 1 Jan 2026
SB 942 (2024) — California AI Transparency Act
In effect

This bill requires providers of generative AI systems with over 1M users / visitors to provide AI detection tools and include persistent latent/manifest watermarks in AI-generated content.

Effective: 2 Aug 2026

Colorado

2 frameworks
SB 24-205 (2024) — Colorado AI Act (CAIA)
In effect

The Colorado AI Act puts in place obligations for developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems with the aim of preventing algorithmic discrimination. These include transparency, impact assessment and risk management obligations for deployers, and documentation requirements for both developers and deployers of high-risk AI systems. It should be noted that the Colorado AI Act is likely to be subject to substantial changes regarding scope and obligations, following the recommendations put forward by the Colorado AI Policy Work Group.

At the time of writing this report, the work conducted by the Colorado AI Policy Work Group is still ongoing. Additional information can be found at https://governorsoffice.colorado.gov/governor/news/colorado-artificial-intelligence-policy-workgroup-delivers-unanimous-support-revised-policy

Effective: 30 Jun 2026
SB 25B-004 (2026)
Amendment

This bill amends the Colorado AI Act, extending the enforcement date from February 2026 to June 2026.

Effective: 30 Jun 2026 Amends: SB 24-205

New York

2 frameworks
A6453B (2025) — Responsible AI Safety and Education Act (RAISE Act)
In effect

The RAISE Act puts in place safety obligations for large developers, that is, developers who used > 10²⁶ FLOPs and > $1M in compute. This bill has been amended by S8828, which will enter into force in January 2027.

Effective: 19 Mar 2026
S8828 (2025)
Amendment

This bill amends the RAISE Act, aligning it more closely with California SB 53 through the introduction of frontier developer / large frontier developer thresholds. When compared with A6453B, obligations for large frontier developers have been expanded through a more comprehensive risk management framework and periodic reporting of catastrophic risk assessment.

Effective: 1 Jan 2027 Amends: A6453B

Texas

1 framework
HB 149 (2025) — Texas Responsible Artificial Intelligence Governance Act (TRAIGA)
In effect

TRAIGA puts in place obligations for developers and deployers of AI systems centred on technical documentation to prevent algorithmic discrimination. Additional obligations apply to government agencies and healthcare service providers. Developers and deployers significantly aligned with national risk management standards are exempt from liability. Liability applies if "intent to discriminate" is verified.

Effective: 1 Jan 2027

Utah

2 frameworks
SB 149 (2024) — Artificial Intelligence Policy Act (AIPA)
In effect

AIPA puts in place obligations for suppliers of generative AI systems centred on transparency. All suppliers of generative AI systems must disclose AI interaction upon individual request, while those operating in high-risk domains (i.e., regulated occupations) must do so at the start of the interaction.

Effective: 1 May 2024
SB 226 (2025)
Amendment

This bill amends AIPA. It limits disclosure requirements through a narrower definition of generative AI system and requiring disclose of AI interactions only if interaction is deemed high-risk (e.g., sensitive data, personalised recommendations), in the context of regulated occupations.

Effective: 7 May 2025 Amends: SB 149 (AIPA)

Methodology

The Digital AI Lexicon follows a six-step research methodology. Each step documents a decision in scoping, selecting, validating and analysing the regulatory corpus.

01 Surveying 02 Selecting 03 Identifying 04 Validating 05 Analysing 06 Incorporating
01

Step 1 · Surveying existing U.S. federal and state-level regulations

Research was conducted to identify relevant developments in the U.S. regulatory landscape with regard to Artificial Intelligence, targeting, in particular, the period between April 2024 (date of publication of the previous edition of the EU-U.S. Terminology and Taxonomy for Artificial Intelligence) and March 2026. Four main points were identified:

  1. The landscape of U.S. federal-level regulations is currently limited, particularly following EO 14179 (2025) – see introduction.
  2. Apart form EO 14179, other executive orders have been issued, addressing, for example, “Woke AI” and the “Export of the American AI Technology Stack”, among others. However, these are beyond scope of the current terminology analysis, due the lack of comparative basis with the EU AI Act.
  3. There has been a surge in state-level AI regulations. Several state-level AI regulations have been approved and entered into force, with others at Senate and Assembly levels.
  4. Although multiplying at state-level, the AI regulatory landscape in the U.S. might be impacted in the near future by EO 14365 and the U.S. AI Framework.
02

Step 2 · Selecting relevant U.S. federal and state-level regulations

Following the research conducted to identify relevant AI regulatory frameworks, this study will focus on U.S. state-level regulations due to the scarcity and limited terminological relevance of existing federal AI regulations. Given the volume of U.S. state-level AI regulations, a rationale for selecting relevant U.S. regulations was developed to narrow the number of regulations for analysis in the context of this study. The rationale was the following:

  1. Prioritise regulation that has been passed into law and / or is currently in effect.
  2. Prioritise state regulations that have a comprehensive, cross-sectoral AI governance scope, both across and within states.

This step concluded with the selection of nine U.S. state laws to be included in the comparative analysis, as listed in section 3. These laws span five U.S. states, namely, California, Colorado, New York, Texas and Utah, covering the Republican and Democratic political spectrum.

03

Step 3 · Identifying and selecting relevant terms

Following the selection of U.S. state regulation, the relevant terms to be included in this study were identified and selected. The identification of relevant terms proceeded as follows:

  1. Identify key terms in the EU AI Act – the baseline for comparative analysis – particularly in Articles 3, 17, 52 and Annex III.
  2. Identify key terms in selected U.S. state regulations.
  3. Map and assess high-level overlap of terms identified in U.S. state regulations and the AI Act.
  4. Extract terms and clusters from the second edition of the EU-U.S. Terminology and Taxonomy for Artificial Intelligence (2024).
  5. Cross-reference the Commission Guidelines on the scope of obligations for providers of general-purpose AI models (C(2025) 7719, 19 Nov 2025; cited as "(GL)" in the comparative analysis).

Based on apparent overlaps between concepts, their relevance to understanding regulatory texts, and their absence from the previous edition of the EU-U.S. Terminology and Taxonomy for Artificial Intelligence (2024), 43 terms have been identified.

04

Step 4 · Validating selection of relevant terms

The selection of U.S. state laws as well as relevant terms for comparative EU-U.S. analysis have been presented at a coordination meeting between CEPS and the European Commission. Following the meeting, the European Commission validated the terms selected for inclusion in the current study.

05

Step 5 · Conducting comparative terminology analysis

The comparative analysis was conducted by collecting for each term and from the respective regulatory text, the definition, scope and obligations. These elements formed the basis of the analysis and interpretative notes provided alongside the comparative terminology tables.

06

Step 6 · Incorporating comparative analysis into the Digital AI Lexicon

The terminology tool, named Digital AI Lexicon, was developed alongside the comparative analysis described in step 5, allowing for a reflexive process to improve these two elements of the study. The tool was developed by CEPS Data Science Team with recourse to expertise and generative AI software, which was used to create the basic architecture and interface of the web-based tool.

About this study

The Digital AI Lexicon was produced by the Centre for European Policy Studies (CEPS) in the context of the EU–US Trade and Technology Dialogue (TTD), funded by the European Commission.

Study reference · NDICI FPN FPI/2022/432-762
